subject: Economics
contextNote: JC1 Economics, 24 students. You modelled PED = %ΔQ / %ΔP using a worked example: price rises from $10 to $12, quantity demanded falls from 100 to 80 units. You showed the full calculation on the board and linked the result (PED = −1.0) to the graph — a moderately elastic demand curve. Students are now 25 minutes into three calculation questions. The room looks engaged, but the work tells a different story.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: All 3 calculations correct. Already writing notes on policy implications — tax incidence, revenue effects.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Q1 correct direction (demand fell, price rose) but calculation wrong. Has drawn the graph correctly.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Can explain 'elastic vs inelastic' verbally but has inverted the formula — written %ΔP / %ΔQ instead of %ΔQ / %ΔP.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Written a clear verbal explanation of elasticity. Calculation space completely blank. Has not attempted the numbers.

ep1.situation: 25 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the pattern is striking — not a readiness gap, but a representation gap. Rajan has all three calculations correct and is already extending to policy: he has written 'inelastic demand → tax burden falls on consumer → revenue rises.' His understanding spans formula, graph, and policy. Nur has drawn the demand curve correctly and labelled it 'elastic' — but her calculation for Q1 gives PED = −0.5 when the correct answer is −1.0. She divided the wrong way. Wei Jie can explain elasticity fluently in words: 'If demand is inelastic, a price rise barely affects quantity demanded.' But his formula is inverted — he has written %ΔP / %ΔQ. His verbal understanding is strong; his symbolic execution is wrong. Siti has written two sentences of verbal explanation that are accurate: 'Elastic demand means consumers are sensitive to price changes. Inelastic demand means they are not.' But her calculation space is completely blank — she has not attempted the numbers at all. A quick scan of the class: 8 students have correct calculations with some policy link. 10 students have attempted the calculation with errors — mostly formula inversion or percentage change errors. 6 students, including Siti, have verbal explanations but no calculation attempt. You have about 20 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The gap is not in conceptual understanding — most students can explain elasticity correctly in words. The gap is in representation: students are strong in one mode (verbal, graphical, or symbolic) but cannot connect across modes. Correct verbal understanding is not translating into correct calculation.

choices.label: Two-step multi-representation diagnostic
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a new data set: price rises from $5 to $6, quantity demanded falls from 200 to 160 units. Ask two questions in sequence: '(1) Calculate PED and show every step of your working.' Then: '(2) How did you approach this? Choose: (a) I used the formula directly. (b) I drew the graph first. (c) I thought about it verbally first, then calculated. (d) I used a different approach.' Ask students to write both answers on paper and hold them up. Use the pattern to see which representation each student is anchored in and where the calculation is breaking down.
choices.tradeoff: The two-step structure reveals both the calculation accuracy and the representational approach, giving you whole-class evidence of where the disconnect sits. But students who are anchored in verbal understanding may feel exposed by the calculation demand. The public display of approaches may embarrass students like Siti who have not attempted the numbers.

choices.label: Tomlinson profile tiers: same data, different entry points
choices.elaboration: Distribute differentiated task cards without stopping the class. All cards use the same data set (price $5 to $6, quantity 200 to 160). Visual learners get a graph with the demand curve drawn and labelled — they must read off the PED and explain what the slope tells them. Symbolic learners get the formula with blanks: 'PED = (%ΔQ / %ΔP) = (___ / ___) = ___.' Verbal learners get a policy scenario: 'A firm is considering a 20% price rise. Explain whether this is a good idea, using PED.' All three cards require students to connect their preferred representation to the others.
choices.tradeoff: Profile-based tiers give every student a dignified entry point into the same content. Students who are strong in one representation can build from their strength toward the others. But the tiers may reinforce single-representation thinking rather than building integration. Students who receive the verbal card may never attempt the calculation, and you lose whole-class evidence of where the formula errors are.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: connect representations in triads
choices.elaboration: Form triads of three students — one anchored in each representation where possible. Ask each triad to explain the same PED result three ways: 'The graph shows ___ because ___. The formula gives ___ because ___. The policy implication is ___ because ___.' Each student leads on their strongest representation and the triad must connect all three. Circulate and listen for triads where the connections are genuine versus scripted. After 4 minutes, run a brief IRF probe with the whole class using the strongest triad's explanation.
choices.tradeoff: Triad talk builds the bridge between representations through peer explanation and is low-stakes for students who are strong in one mode. But the verbal-dominant students may take over the triad, and students like Siti who are anchored in verbal understanding may never be pushed to attempt the calculation. The IRF probe risks exposing students who cannot connect across representations.

A.consequence: The responses come in and the pattern is clear. 16 of 24 students calculated PED correctly (−2.0). But on the representation question, only 9 students chose option (a) — direct formula use. 8 students chose option (b) — graph first. 5 students chose option (c) — verbal first, then calculated. 2 students, including Siti, left the calculation blank and chose option (c) without attempting the numbers. Nur got the calculation wrong (she wrote −0.5) and chose option (b) — she drew the graph correctly but divided the wrong way when she moved to the formula. Wei Jie got the calculation wrong (he wrote −0.5 again, from the inverted formula) and chose option (a) — he went straight to the formula but has it backwards. Rajan got it right and chose option (a), adding a note: 'PED > 1 so elastic — firm should not raise price.' The evidence is now precise: the verbal and graphical understanding is largely correct, but the formula execution is breaking down for students who are not anchored in symbolic reasoning. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: correct the formula errors
choices.elaboration: Address the two most common errors directly with the whole class. Write both errors on the board side by side: 'Common error 1: PED = %ΔP / %ΔQ (inverted). Common error 2: Percentage change calculated as absolute change, not relative change.' Work through the correct calculation step by step: '%ΔQ = (160 − 200) / 200 × 100 = −20%. %ΔP = (6 − 5) / 5 × 100 = +20%. PED = −20% / +20% = −1.0.' Emphasise: 'The formula is always %ΔQ over %ΔP — quantity on top, price on the bottom.' Ask all students to redo Q1 using the correct formula and show every step.
choices.tradeoff: The reteach addresses the specific formula errors precisely and efficiently. It will reach the 10 students who have the formula inverted or the percentage change wrong. But it treats the class as uniform and shifts back to teacher-led instruction. Rajan, who already understands, is under-challenged. Siti, who has not attempted the calculation at all, may watch and copy the board without building genuine understanding of what the formula means.

choices.label: AQS peer feedback on calculation steps
choices.elaboration: Use the diagnostic evidence to form pairs: students who calculated correctly (like Rajan) are paired with students who made formula errors (like Wei Jie) or left the calculation blank (like Siti). The correct student gives AQS feedback on their partner's working: Affirm what is right ('Your verbal explanation is correct — demand is elastic'), Question the formula step ('Why did you put %ΔP on top? What does the formula measure?'), Suggest the correction ('Try writing %ΔQ first — quantity is what we are measuring the response of'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange with the class.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise already in the room. Wei Jie, who has strong verbal understanding, may benefit from having his formula error questioned by a peer rather than corrected by the teacher. But Siti has no calculation to give feedback on — her partner will end up explaining the formula from scratch rather than giving AQS feedback on existing work. Rajan is giving rather than receiving, which may not challenge him.

B.consequence: The profile cards go out and students begin working. Rajan receives the symbolic card, reads it quickly, completes the calculation, and then writes a policy extension unprompted: 'PED = −2.0, elastic, so a price rise would reduce total revenue — firm should not raise price.' He has gone beyond the card. Nur receives the visual card and works through the graph carefully — she reads off the correct PED value and labels the curve 'elastic.' But when you ask her 'Can you write the formula that gives you that number?', she hesitates and cannot. Wei Jie receives the verbal card and writes a strong policy explanation: 'If PED > 1, demand is elastic — consumers are very responsive to price changes, so a tax would reduce quantity demanded significantly.' But his card has no calculation, and he has not attempted the formula. Siti receives the visual card and copies the graph from the board. When you ask her to explain what the slope tells her, she says 'It is elastic' but cannot say more. The profile tiers have given every student a dignified entry point, but the integration across representations is not happening naturally. 19 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF probe: connect representations across the class
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run a cross-representation IRF chain. Ask: 'Nur, what does your graph tell you about PED?' (Initiation — visual). Then ask: 'Wei Jie, can you express what Nur just said using the formula?' (Response — symbolic). Then ask a third student: 'Can you connect both of those to a policy implication?' (Feedback — verbal). Use the chain to build a complete, class-constructed explanation that moves from graph to formula to policy. Write the final chain on the board.
choices.tradeoff: The cross-representation IRF chain makes the connections between modes visible and public. It leverages the different strengths in the room — Nur's graphical understanding, Wei Jie's verbal fluency — and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single representation. But it puts students on the spot by asking them to work in a mode they may not be confident in. Siti, who is anchored in verbal understanding, may freeze if asked to connect to the formula.

choices.label: Self-regulation task: use your preferred rep, then check another
choices.elaboration: Ask all students to complete the following independently: '(1) Use your preferred representation to explain PED for the new data set. (2) Now check your explanation using a different representation — if you used the graph, write the formula; if you used the formula, draw the graph; if you used words, do the calculation. (3) Write one sentence: What did the second representation add or confirm?' Give 5 minutes, then collect the responses.
choices.tradeoff: The self-regulation task builds metacognitive awareness by asking students to move between representations deliberately. Students who are strong in one mode are pushed to check their understanding in another. But students who are anchored in a single representation — like Siti in verbal — may complete step 1 and stall at step 2. The task requires a level of representational flexibility that not all students have yet developed.

C.consequence: The triads begin talking and the room gets louder. Rajan's triad produces a complete chain: Rajan explains the formula, his partner draws the graph, a third student gives the policy implication. The chain is fluent and connected. Nur's triad works through the graph together — Nur leads on the visual, her partners nod. But when the triad tries to write the formula, Nur's partners look at each other and one says 'I think it is %ΔQ over %ΔP?' — uncertain. The formula step is weak in this triad. Wei Jie's triad is dominated by verbal explanation — Wei Jie gives a strong policy argument, his partners agree, and the triad never attempts the formula. Siti echoes her triad's verbal explanation word for word. You circulate and hear the pattern: 7 triads have produced a genuine three-way chain with all representations connected. 11 triads are strong in one or two representations but have not connected all three. 6 triads are verbal-only, with no formula or graph attempted. The talk has partially bridged the representations, but the formula remains the weakest link for most students. 18 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS gallery critique on representation chains
choices.elaboration: Ask each triad to write their three-way chain on a sticky note: 'Graph: ___. Formula: ___. Policy: ___.' Post four notes on the board: one complete chain, one verbal-only, one graph-formula without policy, one with a formula error. Run a whole-class AQS session on the verbal-only note: Affirm the verbal explanation ('The policy reasoning is correct and well-argued'), Question what is missing ('Where is the formula? How do you know the PED value that supports this policy argument?'), Suggest how to complete it ('Try writing PED = %ΔQ / %ΔP = ___ / ___ = ___ and connect the number to your policy claim'). Ask all triads to revise their sticky notes using the AQS feedback.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between a complete chain and a single-representation explanation visible to the whole class. It gives the verbal-only triads a concrete model of what integration looks like. But the triads whose notes are displayed may feel exposed. Siti's triad will likely produce a verbal-only note, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who have not yet attempted the formula.

choices.label: Solo synthesis: graph + formula + policy paragraph
choices.elaboration: Stop the triad talk and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'For the new data set, produce three things: (1) A labelled demand curve showing the PED. (2) The full PED calculation with every step shown. (3) One policy recommendation based on your PED value, with a reason.' Give 5 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who can integrate all three representations independently.
choices.tradeoff: Solo synthesis gives you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's representational integration — uncontaminated by triad talk. Rajan's response will show full integration. But the move to individual work kills the emerging talk momentum. Siti, who was anchored in verbal understanding throughout the triad work, will likely produce a strong policy paragraph and a blank calculation — revealing that the triad talk did not help her bridge to the formula.

A1.outcome: You write both formula errors on the board and work through the correct calculation step by step. The room goes quiet as students watch and copy. Wei Jie looks at the board and immediately sees his error — he has the formula inverted. He erases his working and rewrites it correctly: '%ΔQ = −20%, %ΔP = +20%, PED = −1.0.' He looks relieved. Nur follows the reteach and rewrites her Q1 calculation correctly. She adds a note: 'Quantity on top, price on bottom — I kept mixing them up.' Rajan watches the reteach with visible disengagement — he already understood this and the whole-class explanation does not challenge him. He returns to his policy notes. Siti copies the worked example from the board line by line. When you circulate and ask her 'Can you try Q2 using the same steps?', she looks at the board, copies the structure, and fills in the numbers. Her calculation is correct, but when you ask 'What does PED = −0.5 tell you?', she says 'It is inelastic' — correct, but she cannot connect it to the graph or a policy implication. On a follow-up check, 20 of 24 students now calculate correctly, up from 16. The formula errors have been addressed for most of the class. But the reteach served the middle group and missed the extremes — Rajan is under-challenged and Siti can calculate but cannot integrate. 15 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with worked example is efficient and reaches the majority of students who had formula errors. It is task-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you addressed the specific procedure that went wrong. Wei Jie and Nur made clear gains. But the reteach treats the class as uniform and shifts back to teacher-led instruction. Rajan is under-challenged. Siti can now calculate but the verbal-to-formula integration remains shallow.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose whole-class reteach over peer feedback? What were you prioritising — efficiency, accuracy, or equity?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Wei Jie and Nur corrected their errors, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti can calculate without understanding. What does this tell you about the difference between fixing a formula error and building representational integration?

How do you design feedback that builds the connection between verbal understanding and calculation, rather than just correcting the mechanics?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Wei Jie and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Wei Jie's calculation. Rajan looks at Wei Jie's working and begins: 'Your verbal explanation is correct — demand is elastic. But your formula is inverted. You have %ΔP on top and %ΔQ on the bottom. Try flipping it — quantity is what we are measuring the response of, so it goes on top.' Wei Jie listens, nods, and rewrites the formula. He recalculates and gets PED = −2.0. He says: 'Oh — so elastic means the percentage change in quantity is bigger than the percentage change in price.' Rajan nods. Nur is paired with a student who calculated correctly. Her partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your graph is right — the curve is elastic. But your calculation gives −0.5 instead of −2.0. Check which number goes on top.' Nur rewrites and gets it right. Siti is paired with a student who can explain the formula but struggles to articulate the reasoning. Her partner says: 'You need to write the formula. Just copy: PED = %ΔQ / %ΔP.' Siti copies the formula and fills in the numbers correctly. But when you ask her 'Why is quantity on top?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' On a follow-up check, 18 of 24 students now calculate correctly, up from 16. The peer feedback has activated genuine reasoning for Wei Jie and Nur. But Siti is still copying rather than understanding, and Rajan is giving rather than receiving. 15 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise in the room. Wei Jie and Nur made real gains through structured peer exchange — the feedback was specific and connected to their actual errors. But Siti's partner could not explain the reasoning behind the formula, so Siti remains dependent on copying. Rajan is under-challenged — giving feedback is not the same as being challenged.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class reteach? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Wei Jie and Nur made genuine gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in a diverse-profiles class?

How do you design peer support so that both the helper and the helped are genuinely learning? What would Rajan need to make this a real learning experience for him too?

B1.outcome: You pause the class and run the cross-representation IRF chain. You ask Nur: 'What does your graph tell you about PED?' Nur looks at her demand curve and says: 'The curve is relatively flat — so demand is elastic. A small price change causes a big change in quantity demanded.' You nod and ask Wei Jie: 'Can you express what Nur just said using the formula?' Wei Jie pauses, then says: 'PED equals percentage change in quantity over percentage change in price. If the curve is flat, the percentage change in quantity is bigger — so PED is greater than 1.' You write this on the board. You ask a third student: 'Can you connect both of those to a policy implication?' The student says: 'If demand is elastic, a firm should not raise price — it would lose more revenue from the quantity fall than it gains from the higher price.' The IRF chain produces a complete, class-constructed explanation. Rajan listens with interest — the chain has connected representations in a way that even he finds clarifying. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask her 'Does that make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. On a follow-up check, 16 of 24 students can now connect all three representations, up from 7. 15 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The cross-representation IRF chain makes the connections between modes visible and public. It leverages the different strengths in the room — Nur's graphical fluency, Wei Jie's verbal reasoning — and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single representation. Rajan was unexpectedly engaged by the integration. But Siti did not contribute and the chain did not give her an entry point into the formula. The probe served the middle group well but did not stretch Rajan or support Siti.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run a cross-representation IRF chain rather than a single-representation reteach? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced strong integration for the middle group, but Siti remained passive. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for whole-class discussion to include all students?

How do you use whole-class discussion in a way that includes students who are anchored in a single representation, rather than exposing them?

B2.outcome: Students work through the self-regulation task independently. Rajan completes step 1 (formula) in under 2 minutes, draws the graph in step 2, and writes: 'The graph confirms the formula — a flat curve means a large quantity response to a small price change, which is what PED > 1 shows.' His response shows genuine integration and metacognitive awareness. Nur completes step 1 (graph) correctly, then attempts step 2 (formula). She writes the formula correctly this time — '%ΔQ / %ΔP' — and gets the right answer. Her step 3 says: 'The formula gave me the exact number that the graph showed visually.' Wei Jie completes step 1 (verbal) with a strong policy argument, then attempts step 2 (formula). He writes the formula correctly but makes an arithmetic error in the percentage change calculation. His step 3 says: 'The formula is harder than the words.' Siti completes step 1 (verbal) with a correct explanation, then stalls at step 2. Her step 3 says: 'I don't know how to do the calculation.' The self-regulation task has given you honest individual evidence of representational flexibility. Rajan and Nur have genuine integration; Wei Jie is close but has an arithmetic error; Siti cannot bridge to the formula. 15 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: The self-regulation task builds metacognitive awareness by asking students to move between representations deliberately. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine integration — they could check their understanding in a second mode and articulate what it added. Wei Jie is close but has an arithmetic gap. But Siti stalled at step 2 — the task revealed that she cannot bridge from verbal to formula, and you now have clear evidence of this with 15 minutes remaining.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose a self-regulation task over a whole-class probe? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine integration, but Siti cannot bridge to the formula. What does this tell you about the timing of self-regulation tasks in a diverse-profiles class?

For students who are anchored in verbal understanding, what is the right sequence of support to build the bridge to calculation? How do you design that sequence into the lesson rather than discovering the gap at the end?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the verbal-only note. Several students affirm: 'The policy reasoning is correct and well-argued.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand: 'Where is the formula? How do you know the PED value that supports this policy argument?' You write: 'Question: What is the PED value? How did you calculate it?' A third student suggests: 'Write PED = %ΔQ / %ΔP = ___ / ___ = ___ and connect the number to your policy claim.' You write this on the board. The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all triads to revise their sticky notes. Several verbal-only triads begin attempting the formula for the first time. Wei Jie's triad writes the formula correctly and connects it to their policy argument. Nur's triad adds the formula step and gets the right answer. Siti's triad produces a revised note that still has no formula — they write 'We know demand is elastic' but cannot calculate the PED value. When you circulate and ask Siti's triad 'Can you try the formula?', they look at the board and copy the structure but fill in the numbers incorrectly. 15 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the difference between a complete chain and a single-representation explanation visible to the whole class. It gave the verbal-only triads a concrete model of what integration looks like, and several triads attempted the formula for the first time. But Siti's triad remains stuck — they can see what is missing but cannot produce it. The AQS feedback served the middle group but did not give Siti's triad an entry point into the formula.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Several triads attempted the formula for the first time, but Siti's triad remains stuck. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for AQS feedback to activate new learning rather than just model it?

How do you design tasks that help students who are anchored in verbal understanding move to calculation — without making the formula feel like a separate, harder task?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students work individually on their solo synthesis. Rajan produces all three elements quickly: a correctly labelled demand curve, a full calculation with every step shown, and a nuanced policy recommendation with caveats about other factors. His response shows complete representational integration. Nur produces a correct graph and a correct calculation — she has internalised the formula from the triad work. Her policy paragraph is brief but accurate. Wei Jie produces a strong policy paragraph and a partially correct calculation — he has the formula right but makes an arithmetic error in the percentage change. His graph is missing. Siti produces a strong policy paragraph and a correctly labelled graph — but her calculation space is blank. When you circulate and ask her 'Can you try the formula?', she shakes her head. The solo synthesis has given you honest, individual evidence of representational integration. Rajan and Nur have full integration; Wei Jie has partial integration with an arithmetic gap; Siti has verbal and graphical understanding but cannot bridge to the formula. 15 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Solo synthesis gives you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's representational integration. Rajan and Nur demonstrated full integration — they could produce all three representations independently. But the move to individual work has stopped the emerging talk. Wei Jie's arithmetic gap is now visible but there is limited time to address it. Siti's blank calculation reveals that the triad talk did not help her bridge to the formula — she arrived at the lesson with strong verbal understanding and is leaving with the same gap.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from triad talk to solo synthesis? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed full integration, but Wei Jie has an arithmetic gap and Siti cannot bridge to the formula. What does this tell you about when collaborative talk builds genuine representational integration versus borrowed understanding?

For students like Siti who are anchored in verbal understanding, what is the right sequence of support to build the bridge to calculation? How do you design that sequence into the lesson rather than discovering the gap at the end?
